name: Burmese Mohinga
steps: Simmer the fish in water with the onion until cooked through, about 12-15 minutes, then flake it.
Cook the rice noodles according to package directions.
Combine the fish, its broth, fish sauce, and noodles in a bowl.

ingredients_full.note: sliced, as a banana stem stand-in

steps_detailed: Simmer the fish with the onion, garlic, and turmeric in 2 cups water for 15-18 minutes until the fish is cooked through and falling apart.
Remove the fish, flake it finely, and return it to the broth along with the fish sauce.
Cook the rice noodles according to package directions and drain.
Ladle the fish broth over the noodles, and top with the sliced banana (standing in for the banana stem traditionally used) for a bit of textural crunch.
description: Myanmar's national dish, a fragrant fish and rice noodle soup eaten any time of day, especially breakfast. Real mohinga uses a river fish and the pithy inner stem of the banana plant for texture - a farmed catfish and sliced banana stand in here, offered honestly rather than as if they were the traditional ingredients.

name: Burmese Nan Pyar Thoke (Rice Noodle Salad)

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Toss the cooked noodles with the onion and lime juice.
Top with the crushed peanuts.
Serve at room temperature.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Cook the rice noodles according to package directions, drain, and let cool slightly.
Toss the noodles with the thinly sliced onion, lime juice, and fish sauce.
Top with the crushed peanuts and cilantro.
Serve at room temperature
description: A bright, tangy noodle salad eaten across Myanmar as a light breakfast or snack.

name: Burmese Fried Rice with Egg
steps: Stir-fry the cold rice with the scrambled egg and fish sauce.
steps_detailed: Scramble the egg and set aside.
Fry the garlic and turmeric briefly, then add the cold, day-old rice, breaking up clumps, and stir-fry over high heat 5-6 minutes.
Fold the egg back through, finish with the fish sauce.
description: A simple, everyday fried rice.

name: Burmese Ohn No Khao Swe
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the coconut milk and simmer 20-25 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Cook the noodles separately and ladle the chicken curry over them.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Add the coconut milk and fish sauce, and simmer 22-28 minutes until the chicken is very tender and the sauce has thickened slightly.
Cook the egg noodles separately according to package directions and drain.
Ladle the coconut chicken curry over the noodles and top with sliced hard-boiled egg
description: A beloved Burmese coconut noodle dish, Myanmar's answer to khao soi.

name: Burmese Tea Leaf Salad (Lahpet Thoke)

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Toss the shredded cabbage with the tomatoes and lime juice.
Top with the crushed peanuts.
Season with a little salt and serve.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Fry the garlic in the oil until golden and crisp, then set aside with the oil.
Toss the shredded cabbage with the diced tomatoes and lime juice.
Top with the crushed peanuts and the fried garlic with its oil.
The real dish centers on fermented tea leaves (lahpet), a labor-intensive specialty; this version leans on the same bright, crunchy, savory balance that defines it
description: One of Myanmar's most iconic dishes. The real version centers on fermented tea leaves (lahpet), a labor-intensive specialty that isn't a distinct item here - this version leans on the same bright, crunchy, savory balance that defines it, without the fermented leaves themselves.

name: Burmese Shan Noodles
steps: Brown the ground chicken with the garlic and tomatoes.
Cook the rice noodles and toss with the chicken mixture.
Top with a little of the cooking oil and serve.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken with the garlic, turmeric, and tomatoes in the oil, 8-10 minutes, until the tomatoes have broken down into a light sauce.
Cook the rice noodles according to package directions and drain.
Toss the noodles with the chicken mixture and top with the crushed peanuts.
A specialty of Myanmar's Shan State, this noodle dish is beloved for its simple, savory sauce.
description: A specialty of Myanmar's Shan State, beloved for its simple, savory sauce.

name: Burmese Chicken Salad (Amel Thoke)

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Toss the shredded chicken with the onion and lime juice.
Top with the crushed peanuts and serve at room temperature.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Poach or boil the chicken until cooked through, then shred it finely once cool enough to handle.
Toss the shredded chicken with the thinly sliced onion, lime juice, and fish sauce.
Top with the crushed peanuts and cilantro, and serve at room temperature
description: One of many refreshing Burmese 'thoke' salads built around a central protein.

name: Burmese Pork Curry
steps: Brown the pork with the onion and turmeric.
Add the tomatoes and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 1-1.25 hours until the pork is tender.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the pork in the oil with the onion, garlic, ginger, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and 1/2 cup water. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook over low heat 1-1.5 hours, stirring occasionally, until the pork is completely tender and the oil has started to separate from the sauce, a traditional sign the curry is done.
Serve with steamed rice
description: Burmese curries are typically oil-forward and deeply savory, distinct from their Thai or Indian neighbors - the oil visibly separating from the sauce is a traditional sign the curry is fully cooked, not a mistake.

name: Burmese Chin Baung Kyaw (Sour Leaf Stew)

ingredients.note: chopped, as a sorrel leaf stand-in

steps: Brown the ground chicken with the onion.
Add the chopped watercress and fish sauce, and simmer 8-10 minutes until wilted and tender.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.note: chopped, as a sorrel leaf stand-in

steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken with the onion and garlic in the oil, 6-8 minutes.
Add the chopped watercress (standing in for chin baung, a sour leafy green) and fish sauce.
Simmer 10-12 minutes until the greens are tender and have released their liquid, forming a light sauce.
Serve with steamed rice - a homey, tangy Burmese stew built around sour leaves.
description: A homey, tangy Burmese stew built around sour leaves. Real chin baung is a specific sour leafy green that isn't a distinct item here - watercress stands in, offering some of the same green, slightly bitter character without the real sourness.

name: Burmese Grilled Fish with Turmeric
steps: Rub the fish with the turmeric and garlic.
Grill or pan-fry over medium-high heat, 4-5 minutes per side, until cooked through.
Finish with the lime juice before serving.
steps_detailed: Rub the fish thoroughly with the turmeric, garlic, and fish sauce, and let marinate 15-20 minutes.
Heat the oil in a skillet or grill pan over medium-high heat and cook the fish 4-6 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Finish with a generous squeeze of lime juice before serving
description: Simple, aromatic, and typical of everyday Burmese cooking.

name: Burmese Beef Curry

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion, turmeric, and ginger.
Add a splash of water and simmer, covered, 1.5-2 hours until the beef is tender.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef in the oil with the onion, garlic, ginger, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and 1/2 cup water.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.5-2 hours, stirring occasionally, until the beef is completely tender and the oil separates to the surface, the traditional marker of a finished Burmese curry.
Serve with steamed rice.
description: The oil separating to the surface is the traditional marker of a finished Burmese curry, not a sign of overcooking.

name: Burmese Samosa
steps: Brown the beef and mix with the mashed potato.
Make a simple dough from the flour and water, roll thin, cut into triangles, fill, and fold to seal.
Fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion and turmeric, then mix with the mashed potato.
Make a simple, firm dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and roll out thin.
Cut into strips, fill with the potato mixture, and fold into triangles, sealing the edges well.
Fry in the oil until golden and crisp on all sides, 4-5 minutes.
description: Burmese samosas, likely introduced through Indian trade routes, are now a beloved snack across the country, often served in a light soup called samosa thoke.

name: Burmese Split Pea Fritters
steps: Soak the split peas at least 2 hours, then grind coarsely with a little water into a thick paste.
Mix in the onion and turmeric.
Fry spoonfuls of the batter until golden and crisp, about 3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Soak the split peas in water at least 3-4 hours, or overnight, then drain and grind coarsely (a food processor works well) with a splash of water into a thick, textured paste.
Mix in the onion, garlic, turmeric, and black pepper.
Heat the oil in a skillet and fry spoonfuls of the batter, flattened slightly, 3-4 minutes per side until golden and crisp.
description: A popular Burmese street food snack, crunchy outside and soft within.

name: Burmese Banana Fritters
steps: Mix the flour with a little water and the sugar into a smooth batter.
Dip the banana slices in the batter and fry until golden, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, sugar, shredded coconut if using, and enough water to form a smooth, coatable batter.
Dip the banana slices in the batter, coating evenly.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 2-4 minutes per side, until golden and crisp.
Serve warm - a common, well-loved Burmese street snack.
description: A common, well-loved Burmese street snack.

name: Burmese E-Kya Kway (Fried Dough Sticks)
steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, a pinch of salt, and enough water to form a soft dough.
Rest 20 minutes, then shape into sticks and fry until puffed and golden, about 3-4 minutes.
Serve warm, traditionally dipped in tea or condensed milk.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, sugar, a pinch of salt, and enough water to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Rest 20-30 minutes, covered.
Divide and shape into short sticks, pressing two pieces together lengthwise (the traditional method).
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and fry the sticks 3-5 minutes, turning, until puffed, golden, and cooked through.
Serve warm, traditionally torn and dipped into sweet milk tea or condensed milk for breakfast - a snack shared across much of Southeast and South Asia under different names.
description: Traditionally torn and dipped into sweet milk tea or condensed milk for breakfast - a snack shared across much of Southeast and South Asia under different names.

name: Burmese Semolina Cake (Shwe Yin Aye Style)
steps: Toast the farina in the butter for 2-3 minutes.
Add the coconut milk and sugar, and cook, stirring, 5-6 minutes until thick.
Spread into a small dish and let cool before slicing.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Toast the farina in the butter over medium heat, stirring constantly, 3-4 minutes until lightly golden and fragrant.
Gradually stir in the coconut milk and sugar, cooking and stirring 6-8 minutes until very thick and pulling away from the sides of the pan.
Fold in the raisins.
Spread into a small buttered dish, smoothing the top, and let cool completely before slicing into squares
description: A beloved Burmese semolina cake, called sanwin makin, dense and rich.

name: Burmese Sticky Rice with Coconut
steps: Cook the rice, then stir through the sweetened coconut milk.
steps_detailed: Drain the soaked rice and steam in a cloth-lined steamer basket, 20-25 minutes, until translucent and tender.
Warm the coconut milk with the sugar until dissolved, pour over the warm rice, and let absorb 10 minutes.
Top with the nuts.
description: A simple sweet sticky rice, coconut milk and real glutinous rice giving it the proper sticky, rich texture.

name: Burmese Palata with Condensed Milk
steps: Mix the flour, a little of the butter, and water into a soft, elastic dough. Rest 20 minutes.
Roll out very thin, fold repeatedly to create layers, and pan-fry in the remaining butter and oil until golden and flaky.
Drizzle with the condensed milk before serving.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, sugar, a pinch of salt, and enough water to form a soft, elastic dough. Knead well, coat lightly with oil, and rest at least 30 minutes.
Stretch and fold the dough repeatedly to create thin layers, then coil it into a flat round.
Pan-fry in the butter and remaining oil over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden and flaky, crisping the many layers.
Drizzle generously with the condensed milk before serving
description: Palata, related to the wider paratha family, makes a beloved sweet street-food treat in Myanmar.

name: Burmese Balachaung
steps: Fry the onion and garlic in the oil until deeply golden and crisp, about 8-10 minutes.
Stir in the fish sauce.
Cool and store; use as a savory, crunchy condiment over rice.
steps_detailed: Fry the onion and garlic in the oil over medium heat, stirring frequently, 10-12 minutes until deeply golden brown and crisp - watch closely near the end, as they can burn quickly.
Stir in the fish sauce and turmeric off the heat.
Cool completely and store in a sealed jar; balachaung keeps for weeks and is spooned over rice or noodles as a crunchy, savory condiment - a Burmese pantry staple.
description: Balachaung keeps for weeks and is spooned over rice or noodles as a crunchy, savory condiment - a genuine Burmese pantry staple.

name: Burmese Tamarind Dipping Sauce
steps: Soak the tamarind pulp in warm water 10 minutes, then strain to remove the seeds and fiber.
Stir in the sugar and fish sauce.
Serve alongside fried snacks or grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Soak the tamarind pulp in 3 tablespoons warm water for 10-15 minutes, working it with your fingers to loosen the pulp, then strain through a sieve to remove seeds and fiber.
Stir the sugar, fish sauce, and jalapeno (if using) into the strained tamarind liquid until the sugar dissolves.
Serve alongside fried snacks like samosas and fritters, or grilled meats
description: A tangy-sweet-savory dip found throughout Myanmar, served alongside fried snacks or grilled meats.
